title: The Cloud Weaver
author: Sleep Reader
paragraphs: High above the world, where the air is thin and sweet and the wind speaks in whispers, there lives an old woman who weaves clouds. Her name is Cela, and she has been at her work for longer than anyone can remember. Her loom is made of silver birch and strung with threads of mist, and it sits on a platform of packed snow at the very top of a mountain that has no name on any map. She works slowly, the way all important things are done, one thread at a time.

Each morning, Cela gathers her materials. She walks to the edge of her platform and holds out a wide, shallow bowl made of pale blue stone. The mist rises from the valleys below and pools in the bowl like milk, cool and soft and faintly shimmering. She carries it back to her loom and begins to spin it into thread, drawing it out between her fingers the way a spider draws silk. The thread is so fine it is nearly invisible, but when the light catches it, it glows like a strand of moonlight.

Cela weaves different clouds for different purposes. The high, thin clouds that stretch across the sky like brush strokes are her simplest work, just a few threads pulled wide and left to drift. She makes these on clear mornings when the sky needs only a little decoration, a scarf of white against the blue. They are quick to make and quick to dissolve, lasting just long enough to catch the sunrise and then fading like a thought you cannot quite hold onto.

The large, round clouds that pile up like pillows on a summer afternoon take more time. Cela builds them layer by layer, folding the mist into thick, soft billows and tucking the edges under so they hold their shape. She pats each one gently before releasing it from the loom, the way a baker shapes a loaf before setting it to rise. These clouds drift slowly across the sky, casting cool shadows on the fields below, giving the earth a rest from the warmth of the sun.

But Cela's finest work is reserved for the evening. The clouds that appear at sunset, the ones that turn pink and gold and deep violet, require a special thread that she keeps in a wooden box beside her loom. This thread is spun from the last light of the day, gathered in the hour before dark when the sky is at its most generous. She weaves it into the edges of her clouds, and when the setting sun touches them, they bloom into color like flowers opening in fast motion.

Cela does not work alone. A small grey bird lives on the mountain with her, a bird with no particular name that comes and goes as it pleases. It perches on the frame of the loom and watches her work with bright, patient eyes. Sometimes it sings, a simple melody of four or five notes that repeats like a slow heartbeat. Cela has never asked the bird to sing. It simply does, the way the wind simply blows, and its song has become so woven into her days that silence without it would feel incomplete.

On some evenings, when her work is done and the sky is full of her creations, Cela sits on the edge of her platform and looks down at the world below. The towns and villages are tiny, their lights just beginning to flicker on like the first stars in reverse. She watches the clouds she made that day drift over them, casting soft shadows, carrying rain to gardens that need it, shading a child who fell asleep in the grass. She does not know these people, but she tends to their sky the way a gardener tends to a plot of earth that belongs to everyone.

The grey bird settles on her shoulder. The wind dies down to a breath. Far below, a farmer looks up and sees the evening clouds, their edges lit with gold, and thinks how beautiful the sky is tonight, not knowing that someone made it so. He does not need to know. The beauty is enough. He stands in his doorway and watches until the colors fade, and then he goes inside to his family and his warm kitchen, carrying the peace of the sky with him like something held in cupped hands.

Cela rises from her seat and covers her loom with a cloth of pale grey wool. She banks the small fire in her stone hearth and sets her bowl of mist aside for tomorrow. The grey bird hops from her shoulder to its roosting spot in the rafters, tucks its head under its wing, and is still. The mountaintop is quiet, wrapped in the kind of silence that is not empty but full, the silence of a room after music has ended, when the notes are still settling into the walls.

Above Cela's mountain, the stars appear, one by one, in a sky she has left clear for them. She has woven no clouds for the night. The darkness needs its own beauty, unadorned, and the stars do not need her help to shine. She lies down on her narrow bed, pulls a blanket of cloud-wool to her chin, and closes her eyes. Tomorrow there will be more mist to gather, more threads to spin, more sky to tend. But that is tomorrow. Tonight, the weaver rests.

And far below, the world sleeps under the last of her clouds, soft shapes dissolving slowly into the dark like sugar into warm tea. The sky is gentle. The air is still. Somewhere, a child looks out a window and sees a wisp of cloud passing over the moon and wonders where it came from. It came from the mountain, from the loom, from the quiet hands of a woman who tends the sky so the rest of us can sleep peacefully beneath it. Goodnight, little one. The sky is in good hands. Sweet dreams.
